points: Colonial forest policies in India and Java prioritised commercial needs—railways, shipbuilding, and plantations—over local livelihoods, leading to widespread deforestation and displacement of forest communities.

The introduction of scientific forestry and Forest Acts (1865, 1878, 1927) restricted traditional practices like shifting cultivation, hunting, and collection of forest produce, forcing villagers into labour systems like blandongdiensten in Java.

Forest communities resisted colonial control through rebellions such as the Bastar uprising (1910) and Samin's movement in Java, forcing policy changes and highlighting the deep connection between local people and forest resources.
